Chapter One

White Sands & the Dunes That Keep Calling

White Sands National Park  ·  New Mexico  ·  2016 – 2025

"I have been to White Sands five times. Each time, I needed something different. Each time, it gave it to me."

Read the chapter ›

The first time, I didn't plan it. We were driving from Killeen to Las Vegas — a military family on the move, four kids in the car who didn't want to be there. Someone said there were white sand dunes a few hours off the route. We stopped.

Nobody was prepared for what it looked like. The gypsum is so white it reads as snow. The sky that day was the kind of blue that makes you feel like you're inside a painting. The kids, who had been complaining since Texas, went quiet. Then they started running.

"They went quiet. Then they started running. That's what White Sands does — it stops you and then it sets you free."

Left: The first visit, 2016 — sleds, silhouettes, and a sunset that stopped everyone mid-sentence. Right: 2025 solo — Stop Rescheduling Joy.

I went back in 2017 with my husband and family, right after a deployment. We needed the reset that wide open spaces provide. I went back in 2023 with my niece, her best friend, and their two sons — watching the next generation discover it for the first time.

Christmas 2024, I brought the whole family — adult children, my son-in-law, all of us. Nobody had to be convinced. They asked to go.

2025, I went alone. Pink pants. Linen top. No plan, no agenda, no one to check in with. I jumped on the dunes and stayed until the light changed. That's what five visits looks like. That's what White Sands does — it grows with you.

Wander With Me

When did you last go somewhere just to see what it looked like?

Is there a place you've been meaning to take your family that keeps getting pushed to next year?

What would you do with a whole afternoon in a place that looked like another planet?

Chapter Two

The Road That Changed Everything

Carlsbad Caverns · Painted Desert · Grand Canyon · White Sands  ·  2016

"We left Killeen not knowing we were about to discover what kind of family we actually were — the kind that stops."

Read the chapter ›

It started as a practical decision. We had to get from Killeen, Texas to Las Vegas, Nevada. We had four kids, a car packed too full, and a route that cut straight through some of the most extraordinary landscape in the country.

Someone — I honestly don't remember who — suggested we stop at Carlsbad Caverns. It was 2016, the centennial year of the National Park Service. Every park had a banner up: "100 Years of Learning." We were about to learn something.

Carlsbad Caverns, New Mexico — NPS Centennial, 2016. The sign said "100 Years of Learning." We were paying attention.

Going underground changes you. There's something about descending into the earth — the temperature drop, the way the light disappears, the sheer age of everything around you — that puts the whole surface world in perspective. The kids were wide-eyed the entire time.

We drove back up and kept going west. The Painted Desert appeared like something out of a movie — the reds and purples and golds layered in the distance. We pulled over. We jumped.

"The Painted Desert looks like someone took a brush and decided the earth needed more color. You don't drive past it. You stop."

Painted Desert overlook — arms up, hands joined. The kids who didn't want to stop are now the ones asking to.

Then the Grand Canyon. Nothing prepares you for the Grand Canyon. You know it's large. You've seen pictures. And then you stand at the rim and every reference point you had for large stops working.

I watched someone hold a child up at the edge — pointing toward the canyon like they were showing them the whole world. That's what it felt like. The whole world, cracked open and visible.

Grand Canyon South Rim, 2016. Nothing prepares you. And then you're prepared for everything.

We ended the day at White Sands. That first visit — the one I now count as the beginning of everything. The kids ran down the dunes. We stayed until the sun went down and the dunes turned pink and then gray and then we couldn't see anything except the stars.

We drove into the Nevada dark talking about what we'd seen. Four parks in one day. One road. One family who discovered they were the kind of people who stop for things.
